title: Vietnamese vowels array for telex scanning

content.text:
Based on the spec @specs/lex-vietnamese-telex-input-method.md and production code @src/liblex.zig discuss with me to fill the `vowels` array so that we can use to scan Pseudoword. I already define the placeholder array for this. The array will be used to compare with each Span.base in buffer_effective to detect vowels.

Don't write any code. Don't provide example code.

content.thinking:
**Considering file inspection**

I need to focus on discussing rather than coding and inspect files instead. I have to make sure to follow AGENTS' instructions, unless the user specifically confirms execution. The user is asking for a discussion, not for coding help. I’ll read the specification and look into `src/liblex.zig`. Since using `shell_command` works, I can use `grep` or `cat`, but it seems like `rg` isn't an option here.

content.text: I’ll read the spec and the existing placeholder in `src/liblex.zig`, then discuss the `vowels` content and constraints without editing code.

result.output:
# Lex - Vietnamese Telex input method

The specification of the Lex program, a Vietnamese Telex input method.

## Vietnamese Telex Rules

### Literal input characters

- When any of the follow characters added to the word, change to literal input from the new character position: `F`, `J`, `W` (ignore cases), because these characters don't appear in formal Vietnamese spelling. `Z` existing in Vietnamese word such as _Dzũng_, _Hồ Dzếnh_
- Literal input remains until the new word.
- When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
- This rule is lower priority than diacritics and tones rules.

### Diacritics

Diacritics let a character have many variant. We only focus on character diacritics in this section.
Tones have a separate section.

#### Empty

- Any literal alphabet character has empty diacritic by default.
- Only a specific number of characters have other diacritic and it must follow the rules for those
  characters.

#### Circumflex

- Valid for: `A` (Â), `E` (Ê), `O` (Ô) and their lower cases.

#### Breve

- Valid for: `A` (Ă) and its lower case.

#### Horn

- Valid for: `O` (Ơ), `U` (Ư) and their lower cases.

#### Stroke

- Valid for: `D` (Đ) and its lower case.

#### Apply rules

- Only apply diacritic when the immediate previous character doesn't have any diacritic (empty).
- _Circumflex_, _stroke_ are triggered when user inputs the same character (ignore case) with the
  existing character when that character is valid for the applying diacritic.
  - `Aa` -> `Â`, `Ee` -> `Ê`, `Oo` -> `Ô`.
  - `Dd` -> `Đ`.
- _Breve_, _horn_ are triggered when user input `W`, `w` after the character when that character is
  valid for the applying diacritic. Otherwise, treat `W`, `w` literally.
  - `Aw` -> `Ă`.
  - `Ow` -> `Ơ`, `Uw` -> `Ư`.
- The existing character keep its case, tone.
- Treat out of scope characters as literal.

#### Override rules

- New valid diacritic can override existing valid diacritic of the same base character.
  - `Ăa` -> `Â`, `Âw` -> `Ă`.
  - `Ôw` -> `Ơ`, `Ơo` -> `Ô`.
- Only `A`, `O` have override rules because they have more than one possible character diacritic.
- Keep tone untouched.
- The existing character keep its case, tone.
- Treat out of scope characters as literal.

#### Cancel rules

- The cancellation is triggered if and only if user inputs the character (ignore case) that would
  produce the existing diacritic for the same base character of the immediate previous character.
- The existing character keeps its case, tone, but the diacritic is removed.
  - Circumflex: `Âa` -> `Aa`, `Ốo` -> `Óo`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
  - Breve: `Ăw` -> `Aw`.
  - Horn: `Ưw` -> `Uw`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
  - Stroke: `Đd` -> `Dd`, `đD` -> `dD`.
- The new character is literal, keeps its case, no tone.
- Switch to literal from the new character position and the following characters. Literal input
  remains until the new word.
  - Existing `Â`, input `aa`, result `Aaa`.
- When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.

#### Auto-fill rules

- The auto-fill is only applied for `ươ` syllable immediately before the user input, when one of the
  character in the syllable is missing horn: `uơ` and `ưo`.
- Fill the missing _horn_ when the input is one of these character (ignore case): `C`, `I`, `M`,
  `N`, `P`, `T`, `U` and their lower cases.
  - Fill missing horn on `U`: `uơn` -> `ươn`.
  - Fill missing horn on `O`: `ưóp` -> `ướp`.
- The new input character is appended literally.
- Tone and case of existing characters are preserved on the original characters.

### Tones

Tones let a word have many variants, each variant has different meaning. Only the following
Vietnamese vowel could have tones: `A`, `E`, `I`, `O`, `U`, `Y` and their corresponding variants.
These vowel could take any tone.

#### Level (ngang)

- The default tone, it's the vowel without any tone mark, e.g. `A`, `Ê`, `Ơ`...and all other
  corresponding variants.
- If a vowel has tone mark other than level, use `Z`, `z` to reset the tone to level.

#### Rising (sắc)

- Example of rising tone: `Á`, `Ế`, `Ớ`...and all other corresponding variants.
- Use `S`, `s` to put the rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `As` -> `Á`, `Ês` -> `Ế`, `Ơs`
  -> `Ớ`.

#### Falling (huyền)

- Example of falling tone: `À`, `Ề`, `Ờ`...and all other corresponding variants.
- Use `F`, `f` to put the falling tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Af` -> `À`, `Êf` -> `Ề`, `Ơf`
  -> `Ờ`.

#### Dipping-rising (hỏi)

- Example of dipping-rising tone: `Ả`, `Ể`, `Ở`...and all other corresponding variants.
- Use `R`, `r` to put the dipping-rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ar` -> `Ả`, `Êr` ->
  `Ể`, `Ơr` -> `Ở`.

#### Rising-glottalized (ngã)

- Example of rising-glottalized tone: `Ã`, `Ễ`, `Ỡ`...and all other corresponding variants.
- Use `X`, `x` to put the rising-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ax` -> `Ã`, `Êx`
  -> `Ễ`, `Ơx` -> `Ỡ`.

#### Falling-glottalized (nặng)

- Example of falling-glottalized tone: `Ạ`, `Ệ`, `Ợ`...and all other corresponding variants.
- Use `J`, `j` to put the falling-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Aj` -> `Ạ`, `Êj`
  -> `Ệ`, `Ơj` -> `Ợ`.

#### Apply rules

- Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
  character literally.
- Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
  their lower cases.
- _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
- If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, append new character literally.
- If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and all of them don't have any tone (level), use _tone
  position rules_ to apply the tone for the word.
- If _tone position rules_ could not put tone to the pseudo-word, append new character literally.

#### Cancel rules

- Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
  character literally.
- Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
  their lower cases.
- _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
- If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
- If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has the tone (other than level) on vowels match with
  the triggered tone. Reset that tone to level. Append new character literally.
- If the new character has been appended literally, switch to literal from the new character
  position and the following characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
- When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.

#### Reset rules

- Only trigger reset tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
  character literally.
- Trigger reset tone when user inputs the trigger character for reset: `Z`, `z`.
- _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
- If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has tone (other than level) in one of the vowels,
  change that tone to level, keep case and diacritic.
- If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels or has at least one vowel and all of them don't have
  any tone (level), append the new character literally.
- If the new character has been appended literally, switch to literal from the new character
  position and the following characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
- When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.

#### Override rules

- Only trigger override tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
  character literally.
- Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
  their lower cases.
- _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
- If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, handle by _apply rules_.
- If the pseudo-word has at least one vowel and has the tone (other than level) on vowels different
  from the triggered tone, reset that tone to level, use _tone position rules_ to apply new tone for
  the word. The override tone is always successful.

#### Scan pseudo-word

- Scan pseudo-word is a process to get the information needed from the existing characters to put
  the tone in the correct position.
- The existing characters must not be empty.
- Iterate backward from the last existing character (the new input is the tone trigger character).
- If a vowel is found first, continue go backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no
  character to scan, then stop.
- If a consonant is found first, continue go backward until found the first vowel, then continue go
  backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no character to scan, then stop.
- After iterated, a context have been formed either (in the left to right order, not the reverse)
  `<consonants>`, `<vowels><consonants>`, `<consonant><vowels><consonants>`, `<vowels>` or
  `<consonant><vowels>`.

#### Tone position rules

The process of applying tone to a word go through many steps:

- Focus on the `<vowels>` in peusdo-word, it could be one vowel but could be multiple vowels, the
  tone will be applied on one of these vowels.
- Exceptions:
  - Word exactly `QU` (and their lower cases), could not put tone, this is a consonant only.
- If one vowel, put the tone on that vowel.
- If multiple vowels, follow these rules, priority from top down:
  - Vowels has `Ơ` (and its lower case), put on `Ơ`.
  - Vowels has one any of `Ê`, `Â`, `Ô`, `Ă`, `Ư` (and their lower cases), put on the listed vowels,
    prioritize the first to the last in the list.
  - Word start with `GI`, `QU` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels, put on next vowel, `I` /
    `U` count as part of the consonant.
  - Vowels exactly `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases), put on first vowel.
  - Vowels start with `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels or
    consonants, put on second vowel.
  - Otherwise, put on first vowel.
- Preserve case and diacritic on existing vowels.
- Could not put tone for other cases.

   782	    fn pseudoword(self: *State) Pseudoword {

  1808	test "expect State.add apply rising tone for valid cases" {
  1809	    // Arrange. Each case seeds buffer_effective with spans (all .level tone),
  1810	    // dispatches a rising-tone trigger ('S' / 's'), and asserts which vowel
  1811	    // received the tone. Other spans must keep their seeded base, diacritic,
  1812	    // and .level tone. The pseudo-word scan and tone-position rules from the
  1813	    // spec drive the expected index.
  1814	    const SeedSpan = struct { base: u8, diacritic: Diacritic = .empty };
  1815	    const Case = struct {
  1816	        seeds: []const SeedSpan,
  1817	        trigger: u8,
  1818	        expected_index: u8,
  1819	    };
  1820	    const cases = [_]Case{
  1821	        // `a` -> `á`. <vowels>, single vowel, lowercase trigger.
  1822	        .{ .seeds = &.{.{ .base = 'a' }}, .trigger = 's', .expected_index = 0 },
  1823	        // `A` -> `Á`. <vowels>, single vowel, uppercase trigger.
  1824	        .{ .seeds = &.{.{ .base = 'A' }}, .trigger = 'S', .expected_index = 0 },
  1825	        // `oa` -> `óa`. <vowels>, exact OA -> tone on first vowel `o`.
  1826	        .{ .seeds = &.{ .{ .base = 'o' }, .{ .base = 'a' } }, .trigger = 's', .expected_index = 0 },
  1827	        // `uy` -> `úy`. <vowels>, exact UY -> tone on first vowel `u`.
  1828	        .{ .seeds = &.{ .{ .base = 'u' }, .{ .base = 'y' } }, .trigger = 's', .expected_index = 0 },
  1829	
  1830	        // `ba` -> `bá`. <consonant><vowels>, single vowel.
  1831	        .{ .seeds = &.{ .{ .base = 'b' }, .{ .base = 'a' } }, .trigger = 's', .expected_index = 1 },
  1832	        // `hoa` -> `hóa`. <consonant><vowels>, exact OA -> tone on first vowel `o`.
  1833	        .{ .seeds = &.{ .{ .base = 'h' }, .{ .base = 'o' }, .{ .base = 'a' } }, .trigger = 's', .expected_index = 1 },
  1834	        // `huy` -> `húy`. <consonant><vowels>, exact UY -> tone on first vowel `u`.
  1835	        .{ .seeds = &.{ .{ .base = 'h' }, .{ .base = 'u' }, .{ .base = 'y' } }, .trigger = 's', .expected_index = 1 },
  1836	        // `gia` -> `giá`. <consonant><vowels>, GI special -> `i` joins consonant, tone on `a`.
  1837	        .{ .seeds = &.{ .{ .base = 'g' }, .{ .base = 'i' }, .{ .base = 'a' } }, .trigger = 's', .expected_index = 2 },
  1838	        // `qua` -> `quá`. <consonant><vowels>, QU special -> `u` joins consonant, tone on `a`.
  1839	        .{ .seeds = &.{ .{ .base = 'q' }, .{ .base = 'u' }, .{ .base = 'a' } }, .trigger = 's', .expected_index = 2 },
  1840	
  1841	        // `an` -> `án`. <vowels><consonants>, single vowel.
  1842	        .{ .seeds = &.{ .{ .base = 'a' }, .{ .base = 'n' } }, .trigger = 's', .expected_index = 0 },
  1843	        // `oan` -> `oán`. <vowels><consonants>, extended OA -> tone on second vowel `a`.
  1844	        .{ .seeds = &.{ .{ .base = 'o' }, .{ .base = 'a' }, .{ .base = 'n' } }, .trigger = 's', .expected_index = 1 },
  1845	        // `uyn` -> `uýn`. <vowels><consonants>, extended UY -> tone on second vowel `y`.
  1846	        .{ .seeds = &.{ .{ .base = 'u' }, .{ .base = 'y' }, .{ .base = 'n' } }, .trigger = 's', .expected_index = 1 },
  1847	
  1848	        // `ban` -> `bán`. <consonant><vowels><consonants>, single vowel.
  1849	        .{ .seeds = &.{ .{ .base = 'b' }, .{ .base = 'a' }, .{ .base = 'n' } }, .trigger = 's', .expected_index = 1 },
  1850	        // `quan` -> `quán`. <consonant><vowels><consonants>, QU special -> tone on `a`.
  1851	        .{ .seeds = &.{ .{ .base = 'q' }, .{ .base = 'u' }, .{ .base = 'a' }, .{ .base = 'n' } }, .trigger = 'S', .expected_index = 2 },
  1852	        // `gian` -> `gián`. <consonant><vowels><consonants>, GI special -> tone on `a`.
  1853	        .{ .seeds = &.{ .{ .base = 'g' }, .{ .base = 'i' }, .{ .base = 'a' }, .{ .base = 'n' } }, .trigger = 's', .expected_index = 2 },
  1854	        // `hoan` -> `hoán`. <consonant><vowels><consonants>, extended OA -> tone on second vowel `a`.
  1855	        .{ .seeds = &.{ .{ .base = 'h' }, .{ .base = 'o' }, .{ .base = 'a' }, .{ .base = 'n' } }, .trigger = 's', .expected_index = 2 },
  1856	        // `huynh` -> `huýnh`. <consonant><vowels><consonants>, extended UY -> tone on second vowel `y`.
  1857	        .{ .seeds = &.{ .{ .base = 'h' }, .{ .base = 'u' }, .{ .base = 'y' }, .{ .base = 'n' }, .{ .base = 'h' } }, .trigger = 'S', .expected_index = 2 },
  1858	
  1859	        // `uơp` -> `uớp`. Ơ priority -> tone on `ơ` over plain `u`.
  1860	        .{ .seeds = &.{ .{ .base = 'u' }, .{ .base = 'o', .diacritic = .horn }, .{ .base = 'p' } }, .trigger = 's', .expected_index = 1 },
  1861	        // `tiên` -> `tiến`. Ê priority -> tone on `ê` over plain `i`.
  1862	        .{ .seeds = &.{ .{ .base = 't' }, .{ .base = 'i' }, .{ .base = 'e', .diacritic = .circumflex }, .{ .base = 'n' } }, .trigger = 's', .expected_index = 2 },
  1863	        // `dâu` -> `dấu`. Â priority -> tone on `â` over plain `u`.
  1864	        .{ .seeds = &.{ .{ .base = 'd' }, .{ .base = 'a', .diacritic = .circumflex }, .{ .base = 'u' } }, .trigger = 's', .expected_index = 1 },
  1865	        // `tuôn` -> `tuốn`. Ô priority -> tone on `ô` over plain `u`.
  1866	        .{ .seeds = &.{ .{ .base = 't' }, .{ .base = 'u' }, .{ .base = 'o', .diacritic = .circumflex }, .{ .base = 'n' } }, .trigger = 's', .expected_index = 2 },
  1867	        // `loăt` -> `loắt`. Ă priority -> tone on `ă` over plain `o`.
  1868	        .{ .seeds = &.{ .{ .base = 'l' }, .{ .base = 'o' }, .{ .base = 'a', .diacritic = .breve }, .{ .base = 't' } }, .trigger = 's', .expected_index = 2 },
  1869	        // `tưu` -> `tứu`. Ư priority -> tone on `ư` over plain `u`.
  1870	        .{ .seeds = &.{ .{ .base = 't' }, .{ .base = 'u', .diacritic = .horn }, .{ .base = 'u' } }, .trigger = 's', .expected_index = 1 },
  1871	        // `ăê` -> `ăế`. Ê before Ă in Ê/Â/Ô/Ă/Ư ordering -> tone on `ê`.
  1872	        .{ .seeds = &.{ .{ .base = 'a', .diacritic = .breve }, .{ .base = 'e', .diacritic = .circumflex } }, .trigger = 's', .expected_index = 1 },
  1873	
  1874	        // `thethuy` -> `thethúy`. Suffix pseudo-word `huy`, exact UY -> tone on `u`.
  1875	        .{ .seeds = &.{ .{ .base = 't' }, .{ .base = 'h' }, .{ .base = 'e' }, .{ .base = 't' }, .{ .base = 'h' }, .{ .base = 'u' }, .{ .base = 'y' } }, .trigger = 's', .expected_index = 5 },
  1876	        // `vanhoa` -> `vanhóa`. Suffix pseudo-word `hoa`, exact OA -> tone on `o`.
  1877	        .{ .seeds = &.{ .{ .base = 'v' }, .{ .base = 'a' }, .{ .base = 'n' }, .{ .base = 'h' }, .{ .base = 'o' }, .{ .base = 'a' } }, .trigger = 's', .expected_index = 4 },
  1878	        // `baoquan` -> `baoquán`. Suffix pseudo-word `quan`, QU special -> tone on `a`.
  1879	        .{ .seeds = &.{ .{ .base = 'b' }, .{ .base = 'a' }, .{ .base = 'o' }, .{ .base = 'q' }, .{ .base = 'u' }, .{ .base = 'a' }, .{ .base = 'n' } }, .trigger = 's', .expected_index = 5 },
  1880	        // `bogian` -> `bogián`. Suffix pseudo-word `gian`, GI special -> tone on `a`.
  1881	        .{ .seeds = &.{ .{ .base = 'b' }, .{ .base = 'o' }, .{ .base = 'g' }, .{ .base = 'i' }, .{ .base = 'a' }, .{ .base = 'n' } }, .trigger = 's', .expected_index = 4 },
  1882	    };

content.thinking:
**Evaluating consonants and vowels**

I’m thinking about the consonants array and whether it’s needed if non-vowels are already considered consonants. The spec mentions using the literal alphabet, which is A-Z, so this could mean consonants are strictly non-vowels. The term "first consonant" seems to suggest focusing on alphabetic characters rather than vowels. State.add is asserting an alphabet, but I wonder if mentioning a vowels array could clarify that anything in the alphabet not listed can still be a consonant, including certain marked characters.

content.text:
For `vowels`, I would make it represent **only the six Vietnamese vowel base letters**, not every rendered vowel variant.

Reason: in production, `Span` decomposes a Vietnamese character into:

- `Span.base`: ASCII alphabet base character.
- `Span.diacritic`: empty / circumflex / horn / breve / stroke.
- `Span.tone`: level / sắc / huyền / hỏi / ngã / nặng.

So `Ă`, `Â`, `Ấ`, etc. are still `base = A`; `Ê` is still `base = E`; `Ơ` and `Ô` are still `base = O`; `Ư` is still `base = U`. This is already enforced in [tone validation](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L71-L79) and the test matrix for all toneable vowel forms in [Span.init_diacritic_tone tests](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L158-L185).

Conceptually, the vowel set should be:

- `A`
- `E`
- `I`
- `O`
- `U`
- `Y`

Case handling is the only design choice.

If pseudoword scan will compare directly against `Span.base` without normalizing case, then the array needs both uppercase and lowercase forms: `A E I O U Y a e i o u y`.

If pseudoword scan will normalize `Span.base` before lookup, then the array only needs uppercase forms: `A E I O U Y`.

I prefer **uppercase-only plus normalization before lookup**, because the rest of the production code already treats base comparisons case-insensitively through `equals_ignore_case_and_tone` and `equals_base` in [Span comparison helpers](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L85-L98). That keeps the source of truth smaller and avoids duplicating lowercase values. But if your immediate goal is a minimal direct array membership check against each `Span.base`, then include both cases.

What should **not** go into `vowels`:

- Do not include diacritic-specific variants separately. `Ă`, `Â`, `Ê`, `Ô`, `Ơ`, `Ư` are represented by base plus `diacritic`, not by different `base` values.
- Do not include `D`/`Đ`; stroke is a consonant diacritic, not a vowel.
- Do not include Telex trigger letters like `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J`, `Z`, `W`; they are input triggers, not vowel bases.
- Do not encode tone-position priorities in this array. Priority for `Ơ`, then `Ê Â Ô Ă Ư`, then `GI`/`QU`, `OA`/`OE`/`OO`/`UY`, etc. belongs in tone-position logic, not vowel detection. The array should answer only “is this span’s base a vowel?” The spec separates scan from tone positioning in [apply rules](file:///<redacted>/lex/specs/lex-vietnamese-telex-input-method.md#L133-L143), [scan pseudo-word](file:///<redacted>/lex/specs/lex-vietnamese-telex-input-method.md#L183-L194), and later tone-position rules.

One related implementation constraint: when using this array in `pseudoword`, scan only initialized spans, i.e. up to `buffer_length`, not the entire fixed `buffer_effective` capacity. The current stub starts from `buffer_effective.len - 1` in [pseudoword](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L781-L793), but unused entries are undefined. That matters before vowel detection even runs.

So my recommendation:

- Semantically, `vowels` is the six base vowels: `A E I O U Y`.
- Prefer storing only uppercase forms if scan normalizes `Span.base`.
- Store both cases only if scan does direct byte membership against unnormalized `Span.base`.
Now list the consonants, basically the characters are not vowels, from English alphabet.

The consonants from the English alphabet are all alphabet letters except `A E I O U Y`.

Uppercase set:

`B C D F G H J K L M N P Q R S T V W X Z`

Lowercase set:

`b c d f g h j k l m n p q r s t v w x z`

If used like `vowels`, the same case-handling choice applies:

- Prefer uppercase-only if scan normalizes `Span.base` before lookup.
- Include both uppercase and lowercase if scan compares directly against `Span.base`.

Important detail for this Telex engine: `Y` should be treated as a vowel, not a consonant, because the spec says tones apply to `A E I O U Y` and variants. `W` is a consonant by “not vowel from English alphabet” classification, but in Telex it is also a diacritic trigger / literal-mode trigger, so whether pseudoword scan should ever classify `W` as a consonant depends on whether literal mode has already prevented tone processing from reaching that scan.
Should I put these constants in Pseudoword struct?

I would not put `vowels` / `consonants` inside `Pseudoword`.

`Pseudoword` is currently a **result/data shape**: it stores ranges discovered by scanning `State.buffer_effective`:

- `word_start`
- `word_end`
- `vowels_start`
- `vowels_end`
- `length`

The vowel/consonant sets are **classification rules used to build** a `Pseudoword`, not properties of one pseudoword instance. Keeping them outside the struct is cleaner.

Better placement options:

1. **File-scope constants near `Pseudoword`**  
   This is the best fit for the current design. They are shared lexical facts used by pseudoword scanning and later tone-position logic.

2. **Private helpers near `pseudoword`**  
   If the classification becomes more than a raw membership check, e.g. “is this `Span` a vowel?” and “is this `Span` a consonant?”, helpers near the scan logic may be better than exposing raw arrays.

3. **Inside `Pseudoword` only if it becomes a namespace for pseudoword scanning behavior**  
   For example, if `Pseudoword` later owns methods like scan/classify/tone-position helpers. But with the current struct as plain range information, constants inside it would mix static language tables with per-scan result fields.

My recommendation: keep them at file scope near the placeholder constants, before `Pseudoword`. This matches their role as module-level Vietnamese/ASCII classification tables.
I found `std.mem.indexOfScalar` is good to detect a character is in a category, how is your opinion?

`std.mem.indexOfScalar` is a good fit here.

For these tiny fixed character sets, it is simple, readable, and already used in the production code for similar membership logic: the `W/w` branch checks whether the previous base is one of `AOU` using `std.mem.indexOfScalar` in [src/liblex.zig](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L718-L719).

Why it fits:

- The categories are small: vowels are 6 uppercase letters, consonants are 20 uppercase letters.
- The lookup cost is trivial compared with the rest of input handling.
- It avoids extra tables, bitsets, or switch blocks.
- It keeps the code close to the current style.

I would use it with normalized uppercase input, not duplicate lowercase entries. Conceptually:

- Normalize `Span.base` to uppercase.
- Search in the uppercase category string/array.
- Non-null result means “belongs to category”.

For pseudoword scanning, this also gives clean behavior for diacritic variants because `Span.base` remains `A/E/I/O/U/Y` regardless of `Span.diacritic` or `Span.tone`.

One caution: do not classify by consonants first unless you are certain every scanned span is alphabetic and initialized. Safer logic is usually:

- if base is in vowels: vowel
- else if base is in consonants: consonant
- else unreachable / invalid, depending on invariant

Given `Span.init` asserts alphabetic base, every valid initialized `Span` should be either vowel or consonant under your definitions.
Change the current constants to type fit `std.mem.indexOfScalar`. so that we can use this function to categorize the character later.
I’ll update only the placeholder category constants to explicit `[]const u8` uppercase sets, matching `std.mem.indexOfScalar(u8, category, normalized_base)` usage.

- `vowels` is now `[]const u8`
- `consonants` is now `[]const u8`
- Both use uppercase string literals, suitable for later `std.mem.indexOfScalar(u8, vowels, std.ascii.toUpper(base))`
